title: Statistical Learning
text:
Preface
These are notes for STAT 3113 Regression 2026 Summer at Arkansas Tech University. If you have any questions please contact me through email.
Archived draft material from earlier notes

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Main Topics

What is statistical learning?
Supervised vs unsupervised learning
Parametric vs nonparametric methods
Prediction vs explanation
Bias–variance tradeoff

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Teaching Goals
Students should understand that the main goal of statistical learning is good prediction on new data, not simply fitting the observed sample as closely as possible.

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Suggested Emphasis

Introduce the overall modeling problem first.
Use simple real-world examples such as house prices, medical diagnosis, or spam filtering.
Make overfitting a central theme from the beginning.

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Main Topics

Linear regression as a prediction tool
Interpreting fitted relationships
Strengths and weaknesses of linear models
Linear models as a baseline for comparison

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Teaching Goals
Students should see linear regression as the starting point of predictive modeling: simple, interpretable, and useful, but limited in flexibility.

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Suggested Emphasis

Present linear regression mainly as a predictive model.
Keep inference light unless needed for context.
Highlight when linear models fail to capture nonlinear structure.

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Main Topics

Flexible regression methods
K-nearest neighbors regression
Smoothers and local fitting ideas
Model flexibility and overfitting

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Teaching Goals
Students should learn that more flexible models can better capture complicated patterns, but at the cost of increased variance. ## Suggested Emphasis - Connect this unit directly to bias–variance tradeoff. - Compare flexible and inflexible models visually. - Use plots to show underfitting and overfitting.

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Main Topics

Classification as a prediction problem
Logistic regression
Decision boundaries
K-nearest neighbors classification

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Teaching Goals
Students should understand that classification uses the same modeling logic as regression, but with categorical outcomes. ## Suggested Emphasis - Stress the difference between predicting a class and predicting a numeric response. - Introduce class probabilities early. - Use confusion matrices and classification error as evaluation tools.

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Main Topics

Logistic regression in more detail
Interpreting probabilities
Thresholds and classification decisions
Comparing classifiers

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Teaching Goals
Students should learn how binary classification models convert predictors into class probabilities and final decisions. ## Suggested Emphasis - Distinguish between probability estimation and hard classification. - Discuss how different thresholds change predictions. - Use practical examples such as disease detection or spam filtering.

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Main Topics

LDA
QDA
Naive Bayes
Discriminative vs generative modeling

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Teaching Goals
Students should understand the idea of modeling class distributions directly and then using Bayes’ rule for prediction. ## Suggested Emphasis - Compare logistic regression with LDA and QDA conceptually. - Emphasize assumptions and when they matter. - Use side-by-side model comparisons.

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Main Topics

Validation set approach
Cross-validation
Bootstrap
Estimating test error

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Teaching Goals
This unit is the conceptual center of the course. Students must understand that training error is not enough, and that model performance must be evaluated using principled estimates of test error. ## Suggested Emphasis - Make cross-validation a central practical tool. - Show why naive comparison by training error is misleading. - Use repeated examples across multiple methods.

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Main Topics

Ridge regression
Lasso
Elastic net
Shrinkage and complexity control

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Teaching Goals
Students should understand how regularization controls complexity and improves prediction without exhaustively searching across models. ## Suggested Emphasis - Present regularization as a modern answer to overfitting in linear models. - Compare subset selection and shrinkage conceptually. - Use coefficient path plots when possible.

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Main Topics

Decision trees
Bagging
Random forests
Boosting

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Teaching Goals
Students should learn that combining many unstable or weak learners can create a stronger predictive model. ## Suggested Emphasis - Start with a single tree and its limitations. - Then motivate bagging as variance reduction. - Finally introduce random forests and boosting as improved ensemble ideas.

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Main Topics

End-to-end modeling workflow
Data preprocessing
Model tuning
Model comparison
Practical considerations on real datasets

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Teaching Goals
Students should be able to complete a full predictive analysis from data setup through final model comparison and interpretation. ## Suggested Emphasis - Organize this unit around a full case study. - Show how preprocessing, resampling, tuning, and comparison fit together. - Emphasize reproducible workflow and communication.

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Midterm I
Suggested coverage:

foundations of statistical learning,
linear regression,
nonparametric regression,
early classification concepts.

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Midterm II
Suggested coverage:

classification,
generative models,
resampling,
regularization.

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Final Assessment or Project
Suggested format:

a real dataset,
several competing methods,
model comparison using validation or cross-validation,
written interpretation of results.

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Lecture 2

implementation,
model fitting,
comparison with earlier methods,
short applied example.

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Theme 1: Model Flexibility
Students should repeatedly ask: > How flexible is this model, and what does that flexibility buy us? ## Theme 2: Generalization Students should repeatedly ask: > How do we know whether this model will work on new data? ## Theme 3: Comparison Students should repeatedly ask: > Is this method better than what we already had? # Recommended Course Message to Students A useful recurring message for students is: > Statistical learning is about building models that perform well on new data, understanding the tradeoff between simplicity and flexibility, and choosing methods based on predictive performance. # Condensed Teaching Calendar

Topic
Main Goal

2

Learn to estimate test error


8

Control complexity and improve prediction


9
Ensemble Methods
Combine models to improve performance


10
ML in Practice
Execute a complete workflow

title: Statistical Learning
text:
3. Diagnostics & Residuals
In R, plot(fit) automatically generates four diagnostic plots. In Python, you access these via the .resid attribute or statsmodels.graphics.








Metric / Plot
R Code
Python Code




Residuals
residuals(fit)
fit.resid


Fitted Values
fitted(fit)
fit.fittedvalues


Q-Q Plot
qqnorm(fit$resid)
sm.qqplot(fit.resid, line='s')

plot(fit, which=4)
sm.graphics.influence_plot(fit)


Residual Plot
plot(fit, which=1)
sns.residplot(x=fit.fittedvalues, y=df['y'])

title: Statistical Learning
text:
4. Required Libraries for the Course
To set up a Python environment equivalent to the R statistical suite, run:

title: Statistical Learning
text:
References


[1] Wilkinson, G. N.
and Rogers, C. E. (1973). Symbolic
descriptions of factorial models for analysis of variance. Applied
Statistics 22 392–9.


[2] Chambers, J. M.
and Hastie, T. J. (1992). Statistical

title: 1  Statistical Learning Basics
text:
1.1 What Is Statistical Learning?
Statistical learning studies how to use data to understand relationships and make predictions. In most applications, we observe a response variable \(Y\) and a set of predictors \(X = (X_1, \dots, X_p)\), and we want to learn how \(Y\) depends on \(X\).
A common abstract representation is
\[
Y = f(X) + \varepsilon,
\]
where \(f\) is the systematic part of the relationship and \(\varepsilon\) is random noise.
The main goals are usually:

title: 1  Statistical Learning Basics
text:
prediction: accurately predict the response for new observations;
inference or explanation: understand how the predictors are related to the response.

title: 1  Statistical Learning Basics
1  Statistical Learning Basics
text:
1.3 Regression and Classification
Within supervised learning, two major tasks are:

regression, where the response is quantitative;
classification, where the response is categorical.

For example:

predicting salary is a regression problem;
predicting whether a tumor is benign or malignant is a classification problem.

title: 1  Statistical Learning Basics
text:
1.4 Prediction Versus Inference
Prediction and inference are related, but they are not the same.
If the goal is prediction, we care mainly about how well the model performs on new data. A more complex model may be acceptable if it improves predictive accuracy.
If the goal is inference, we care about understanding the role of each predictor. In this case, interpretability is often more important than pure predictive performance.






NoteMain Point



The best predictive model is not always the best explanatory model. Statistical learning often requires choosing between interpretability and flexibility.

title: 1  Statistical Learning Basics
text:
1.5 Parametric and Nonparametric Methods
A parametric method assumes a specific functional form for \(f\). For example, linear regression assumes that
\[
f(X) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p.
\]
This assumption simplifies estimation and interpretation, but it may be too restrictive.
A nonparametric method does not assume a fixed functional form. It is more flexible, but it usually requires more data and may be harder to interpret.
Thus:

parametric models are simpler and easier to interpret;
nonparametric models are more flexible but may have higher variance.

title: 1  Statistical Learning Basics
text:
1.6 Training Error and Test Error
When we fit a model, we can measure how well it matches the observed data. This is the training error.
However, what really matters is performance on new observations. This is the test error.
A model can have very small training error but still perform poorly on new data. This phenomenon is called overfitting.

Underfitting occurs when the model is too simple to capture the signal.
Overfitting occurs when the model is too flexible and starts fitting noise.

Good statistical learning aims to minimize test error, not merely training error.

title: 1  Statistical Learning Basics
text:
1.7 Bias-Variance Tradeoff
A central idea in statistical learning is the bias-variance tradeoff.

A simple model tends to have higher bias but lower variance.
A flexible model tends to have lower bias but higher variance.

As model flexibility increases:

training error usually decreases;
test error often decreases at first, then increases once overfitting begins.

The goal is to find a level of complexity that balances bias and variance well.






ImportantWhy This Matters



Many modern methods can be understood as ways to manage the bias-variance tradeoff. Regularization, cross-validation, and model comparison are all tools for this purpose.

title: 1  Statistical Learning Basics
text:
1.8 A Typical Statistical Learning Workflow
A practical workflow often looks like this:

Define the prediction or explanation goal.
Explore and preprocess the data.
Choose one or more candidate models.
Estimate model performance using validation or cross-validation.
Compare models and select an appropriate final model.
Interpret results and communicate conclusions.

This course will revisit this workflow repeatedly with different methods.

title: 1  Statistical Learning Basics
2  Linear Regression
text:
2.1 Quick Overview
This section offers a brief review of linear regression, with the main emphasis on its Python implementation through examples.
Linear regression is the fundamental supervised learning method for a quantitative response. It models the conditional mean of the response as a linear function of the predictors: \[
y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_p x_p + \varepsilon.
\]
In matrix notation, this can be written as \[
\vb y = \vb X\beta + \varepsilon.
\] Here, \(\vb X\) is the design matrix, whose first column is a column of ones representing the intercept.

title: 2  Linear Regression
text:
2.2 The Ordinary Least Squares Estimator
The most common fitting method is ordinary least squares (OLS). It chooses the coefficient vector \(\beta\) that minimizes the residual sum of squares:
\[
\rss(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2
= \norm{\vb y - \vb X\beta }_2^2.
\]
The OLS estimator is
\[
\hat{\beta}_{\ols} = (\vb X^\top \vb X)^{-1}\vb X^\top \vb y,
\]
provided that \(\vb X^\top \vb X\) is invertible.
Once the coefficients are estimated, the fitted value for observation \(i\) is
\[
\hat{y}_i = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x_{i1} + \cdots + \hat{\beta}_p x_{ip}.
\]
Interpretation is one of the main strengths of linear regression.

The coefficient \(\beta_j\) represents the change in the mean response associated with a one-unit increase in \(x_j\), holding the other predictors fixed.
When interactions are present, a main effect should not be interpreted in isolation. Its meaning depends on the values of the interacting variables.
If the predictors are centered so that a value of 0 corresponds to the average of the original variable, then the main-effect coefficient can be interpreted as the change in the mean response associated with a one-unit increase in \(x_j\), while holding the other predictors at their average levels.
A confidence interval gives a range of plausible values for a coefficient or for the mean response at a given combination of predictors.
A prediction interval is wider than a confidence interval because it must account for both uncertainty in the estimated mean response, and random variation in a future observation.

title: 2  Linear Regression
text:
2.3 Assessing Model Fit
The classical assumptions of a linear regression model are:

The random errors are independent.
The errors follow a normal distribution.
The mean of the error distribution is \(0\).
The variance of the error distribution is constant across observations.

Several tools are commonly used to assess a linear regression fit. These diagnostics do not prove that a model is correct, but they help identify potential problems.

residual plots and partial residual plots,
Q-Q plots,
leverage and influence diagnostics,
summary measures such as \(R^2\), adjusted \(R^2\), and the residual standard error.

title: 2  Linear Regression
text:
2.4 Formula language
The formula language was developed based on the Wilkinson-Rogers notations introduced in [1], and was later implemented in the S language at Bell Labs by John Chambers and colleagues in the 1980s [2]. It has since become the standard way to specify linear models in statistical software such as R and Python (statsmodels).
The following table summarizes the basic syntax and provides examples. Note that it differs slightly from the R version because Python and R use different syntax.







Formula
Meaning




y ~ x
Response \(y\) regressed on predictor \(x\)


y ~ x1 + x2
Include predictors \(x_1\) and \(x_2\)


y ~ .
Include all available predictors in the dataset


y ~ x1:x2
Interaction between \(x_1\) and \(x_2\) only


y ~ x1 * x2
Main effects and interaction (x1 + x2 + x1:x2)


y ~ x1 + x2 + x3 - x2
Include all listed variables except x2


y ~ (x1 + x2 + x3)^2
All main effects and two-way interactions


y ~ I(x**2)
Include a mathematical transformation of \(x\)


y ~ C(group)
Treat group as a categorical variable


y ~ np.log(x)
Include the transformation \(\log(x)\) using a Python function


y ~ 1
Model with intercept only


y ~ x - 1
Remove the intercept









NoteCategorical Predictors



Categorical predictors are represented through indicator (dummy) variables. If a factor has \(k\) levels, the model usually includes \(k-1\) dummy variables.
In Python when using statsmodels to fit linear regression models, categorical variables are automatically converted into dummy variables. To indicate that a variable is categorical, you may either:

explicitly convert the variable in the dataset to a categorical type using .astype("category"), or
use the C() function in the formula language.

title: 2  Linear Regression
text:
2.5 Model Selection
In traditional multiple regression, several competing sets of predictors may be plausible. Model selection compares these models using criteria that balance goodness of fit with model complexity.

2.5.1 Some Criteria
These criteria reward good fit but penalize model complexity.

AIC tends to prefer somewhat larger models.
BIC penalizes complexity more strongly and often prefers smaller models.
Mallows’ \(C_p\) compares a candidate model to a larger reference model.
PRESS uses leave-one-out data to measure test performance.

The first three methods use training results to estimate test performance, whereas PRESS directly approximates the test error through leave-one-out validation. These tools are useful for comparing models, but they should not replace subject-matter reasoning.


2.5.2 Traditional variable selection methods

Best Subset Selection: Best subset selection compares all models of a given size and then chooses the best one according to a criterion such as AIC, BIC, adjusted \(R^2\), \(C_p\), or PRESS. It is conceptually simple, but it becomes computationally expensive when the number of predictors is large.
Stepwise Selection: Stepwise procedures add or remove variables sequentially rather than searching all possible models. These methods are faster than best subset selection, but they are greedy procedures and do not guarantee the globally best model.

title: 2  Linear Regression
text:
2.6 Python Code
The main library for linear regression is statsmodels. We will also use pandas to deal with dataset and seaborn to deal with data visualization.

2.6.1 Setup
As an example we use the public mpg dataset from seaborn to demonstrate the workflow of the standard linear regression.
This is a dataset for cars manufactured between 1970-82 in USA, Europe., and Japan. It was taken from the StatLib library which is maintained at Carnegie Mellon University. The dataset was used in the 1983 American Statistical Association Exposition. More info can be found here.

import seaborn as sns
1sns.set_theme()

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg")
mpg.head()

This table shows that horsepower contains some missing values. We would like to drop them.

mpg = mpg.dropna()

The goal is to predict mpg using the other features of the car. We remove name because it does not provide meaningful information about vehicle performance. We also treat cylinders and origin as categorical variables.
For model_year, it could reasonably be treated as either a numerical or a categorical variable. For simplicity, we treat it as numerical here, since it has 13 unique values and using 13 categories would make the model less parsimonious.

mpg["cylinders"] = mpg["cylinders"].astype("category")
1mpg["origin"] = mpg["origin"].astype("category")
mpg = mpg.drop(columns=["name"])


1

Like mentioned before, we could also indicate categorical variables in the formula using C().




Now we could see the summary of the cleaned dataset. The default describe() will only show the distribution of numerical values. If we want to see categorical variables, we need to add the argument `include=“category”’.

2.6.2 EDA
We mainly use three types of plots for exploratory data analysis.

import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

sns.pairplot(mpg)
1plt.gcf().set_size_inches(14, 9)


1

plt.gcf() returns the current figure object. Once we have the figure object, we can modify its properties, such as resizing the figure or setting the DPI.













Click to expand.

We can use hue to visualize the effect of categorical variables.

sns.pairplot(mpg, hue = "cylinders")









sns.pairplot(mpg, hue = "origin")










To display the correlation matrix heatmap, we first compute the correlation matrix and then use sns.heatmap() to visualize it.

corr = mpg.corr(numeric_only=True)
sns.heatmap(corr, annot=True, cmap="coolwarm", fmt=".2f")
plt.gcf().set_size_inches(8, 8)  









Finally, we may also want to examine the histogram of each variable, although this information is already reflected in the pair plot.

mpg.hist(
    bins=20,  
1    figsize=(10, 8),
2);


1

The hist() method provides a built-in figsize argument, so there is no need to call plt.gcf() in this case.

2

The hist() method returns an array of axes objects, which would normally produce text output. The ; suppresses this output.













2.6.3 Fit a Basic Model

import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

We use smf.ols(formula).fit() to fit a linear model described by the formula, and use .summary() to get the summary table for the model.

import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

fit_linear = smf.ols(
    "mpg ~ horsepower + weight + acceleration",
    data=mpg,
).fit()

print(fit_linear.summary())

                            OLS Regression Results

Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.
[2] The condition number is large, 3.48e+04. This might indicate that there are
strong multicollinearity or other numerical problems.




2.6.4 More complicated models
We could write more complicated formulas to get more complicated models.

fit_poly = smf.ols(
    "mpg ~ horsepower + I(horsepower**2) + weight + I(weight**2) + acceleration",
    data=mpg,
1).fit()

fit_interaction = smf.ols(
    "mpg ~ horsepower * weight + acceleration",
    data=mpg,
2).fit()

fit_categorical = smf.ols(
    "mpg ~ horsepower + weight + acceleration + C(origin) + C(cylinders)",
    data=mpg,
3).fit()


1

Higher degree models

2

Interaction models

3

Models with categorical variables. Note that since we already turn origin and cylinders into categorical variables, we don’t have to add C() here.






2.6.5 Generate a Summary Table

summary_table = fit_categorical.summary2().tables[1].copy()
summary_table["CI 2.5%"] = fit_categorical.conf_int()[0]
summary_table["CI 97.5%"] = fit_categorical.conf_int()[1]

2.6.6 Generate ANOVA Tables: Type I, II, and III
Type I sums of squares depend on the order in which the terms enter the model. Type II and Type III tables are order-invariant under the usual model-coding assumptions, but they answer slightly different questions.

fit_anova = smf.ols(
    "mpg ~ horsepower + weight + acceleration + C(origin)",
    data=mpg,
).fit()

print("Type I ANOVA")
print(anova_lm(fit_anova, typ=1))

print("\nType II ANOVA")
print(anova_lm(fit_anova, typ=2))

print("\nType III ANOVA")
print(anova_lm(fit_anova, typ=3))

2.6.7 Generate a Nested ANOVA Table
Nested ANOVA compares a smaller model with a larger model that contains all of its terms plus additional predictors.

fit_nested_small = smf.ols("mpg ~ horsepower + weight", data=mpg).fit()
fit_nested_large = smf.ols(
    "mpg ~ horsepower + weight + acceleration + C(origin)",
    data=mpg,
).fit()

anova_lm(fit_nested_small, fit_nested_large)

2.6.8 Residual Plots

diagnostics = mpg[["mpg", "horsepower", "weight", "acceleration", "origin"]].copy()
diagnostics["fitted"] = fit_categorical.fittedvalues
diagnostics["resid"] = fit_categorical.resid
diagnostics["student_resid"] = fit_categorical.get_influence().resid_studentized_internal

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4))

axes[0].scatter(diagnostics["fitted"], diagnostics["resid"], alpha=0.6)
axes[0].axhline(0, color="black", linestyle="--")
axes[0].set_xlabel("Fitted values")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Residuals")
axes[0].set_title("Residuals vs fitted")

sm.qqplot(diagnostics["resid"], line="45", fit=True, ax=axes[1])
axes[1].set_title("Normal Q-Q plot")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()










2.6.9 Partial Residual Plots
Component-plus-residual plots help assess whether the linear contribution of a predictor is adequate after controlling for the other variables.

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
sm.graphics.plot_ccpr_grid(fit_categorical, fig=fig)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()










2.6.10 Pair Plots

pair_data = mpg[
    ["mpg", "horsepower", "weight", "acceleration", "origin"]
].sample(n=min(120, len(mpg)), random_state=1)

sns.pairplot(pair_data, hue="origin", diag_kind="hist")
plt.show()










2.6.11 Leverage and Cook’s Distance Plots

influence = fit_categorical.get_influence()
influence_frame = influence.summary_frame()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

axes[0].scatter(influence_frame["hat_diag"], diagnostics["student_resid"], alpha=0.6)
axes[0].axhline(0, color="black", linestyle="--")
axes[0].set_xlabel("Leverage")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Studentized residual")
axes[0].set_title("Residuals vs leverage")

axes[1].stem(
    np.arange(len(influence_frame)),
    influence_frame["cooks_d"],
    basefmt=" ",
)
axes[1].set_xlabel("Observation")
axes[1].set_ylabel("Cook's distance")
axes[1].set_title("Cook's distance")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()










2.6.12 Stepwise Selection
The following simple forward-selection routine uses AIC as the selection criterion. It is easy to implement, but it is still a greedy search rather than a global optimum.

def forward_selection(data, response, candidates):
    remaining = list(candidates)
    selected = []
    current_aic = np.inf
    history = []

    while remaining:
        scored = []
        for term in remaining:
            formula = f"{response} ~ {' + '.join(selected + [term])}"
            fit = smf.ols(formula, data=data).fit()
            scored.append((fit.aic, term, fit))
        scored.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])
        best_aic, best_term, best_fit = scored[0]
        if best_aic + 1e-8 < current_aic:
            selected.append(best_term)
            remaining.remove(best_term)
            current_aic = best_aic
            history.append((best_term, best_aic))
        else:
            break

    return selected, current_aic, pd.DataFrame(history, columns=["added_term", "aic"])


candidate_terms = [
    "horsepower",
    "weight",
    "acceleration",
    "C(origin)",
    "C(cylinders)",
]

selected_terms, selected_aic, stepwise_path = forward_selection(
    mpg, "mpg", candidate_terms
)

print("Selected terms:", selected_terms)
print("Final AIC:", selected_aic)
stepwise_path

2.6.13 Best Subset Selection
Best subset selection fits every model of a given size and records the best one according to a chosen criterion.

def model_diagnostics(fit):
    y = fit.model.endog
    n = fit.nobs
    p = fit.df_model + 1
    sse = np.sum(fit.resid ** 2)
    mse = sse / fit.df_resid
    press = np.sum((fit.resid / (1 - fit.get_influence().hat_matrix_diag)) ** 2)
    return {
        "r2": fit.rsquared,
        "adj_r2": fit.rsquared_adj,
        "sse": sse,
        "mse": mse,
        "aic": fit.aic,
        "bic": fit.bic,
        "press": press,
        "n": n,
        "p": p,
    }


full_formula = "mpg ~ horsepower + weight + acceleration + C(origin) + C(cylinders)"
full_fit = smf.ols(full_formula, data=mpg).fit()
sigma2_full = np.sum(full_fit.resid ** 2) / full_fit.df_resid


def mallows_cp(fit, sigma2_full):
    sse = np.sum(fit.resid ** 2)
    p = fit.df_model + 1
    n = fit.nobs
    return sse / sigma2_full - (n - 2 * p)


subset_terms = [
    "horsepower",
    "weight",
    "acceleration",
    "C(origin)",
]

subset_rows = []
for k in range(1, len(subset_terms) + 1):
    for combo in itertools.combinations(subset_terms, k):
        formula = "mpg ~ " + " + ".join(combo)
        fit = smf.ols(formula, data=mpg).fit()
        row = {"terms": " + ".join(combo), "size": k}
        row.update(model_diagnostics(fit))
        row["cp"] = mallows_cp(fit, sigma2_full)
        subset_rows.append(row)

subset_table = pd.DataFrame(subset_rows).sort_values(["size", "aic"])
best_subsets = subset_table.groupby("size", as_index=False).first()
best_subsets[
    ["size", "terms", "r2", "adj_r2", "aic", "bic", "cp", "press"]
]

2.6.14 Computing \(R^2\), Adjusted \(R^2\), SSE, MSE, AIC, BIC, \(C_p\), and PRESS
These quantities can be read from a fitted model, but it is useful to compute them directly from the residuals and leverage values.

fit_metrics = fit_categorical
y = fit_metrics.model.endog
n = fit_metrics.nobs
p = fit_metrics.df_model + 1

sse = np.sum(fit_metrics.resid ** 2)
sst = np.sum((y - y.mean()) ** 2)
mse = sse / fit_metrics.df_resid
r2 = 1 - sse / sst
adj_r2 = 1 - (sse / fit_metrics.df_resid) / (sst / (n - 1))
aic = fit_metrics.aic
bic = fit_metrics.bic
cp = mallows_cp(fit_metrics, sigma2_full)
press = np.sum(
    (fit_metrics.resid / (1 - fit_metrics.get_influence().hat_matrix_diag)) ** 2
)

pd.Series(
    {
        "R^2": r2,
        "Adjusted R^2": adj_r2,
        "SSE": sse,
        "MSE": mse,
        "AIC": aic,
        "BIC": bic,
        "Cp": cp,
        "PRESS": press,
    }
)

These examples show how the same regression problem can be examined from several angles: fitting increasingly flexible models, testing nested terms, diagnosing influential observations, and comparing candidate models with both classical and predictive criteria.





[1] Wilkinson, G. N. and Rogers, C. E. (1973). Symbolic descriptions of factorial models for analysis of variance. Applied Statistics 22 392–9.


[2] Chambers, J. M. and Hastie, T. J. (1992). Statistical models in s. Wadsworth & Brooks/Cole.

title: 3  Generalized linear models
text:
3.1 Strengths and Limitations
Linear regression is often the first model to try because it is transparent and effective in many settings. However, it has limits.
Strengths:
Limitations:
These limitations motivate later methods such as GLMs, GAMs, ridge regression, and LASSO.

title: 3  Generalized linear models
3  Generalized linear models
text:
3.2 What Is a GLM?
A generalized linear model (GLM) extends ordinary linear regression in two ways:

the response variable does not need to be normally distributed;
the mean of the response is connected to the predictors through a link function.

A GLM has three components:

A response distribution from the exponential family, such as Gaussian, Binomial, or Poisson.
A linear predictor

\[
\eta = X\beta.
\]

A link function relating the mean \(\mu = \mathbb{E}(Y \mid X)\) to the linear predictor:

\[
g(\mu) = \eta = X\beta.
\]
Thus, a GLM keeps the predictor linear in the coefficients, but it allows the response distribution and the mean-response relationship to be more flexible than in ordinary linear regression.






NoteMain Idea



Linear regression models the response directly under a Gaussian error assumption. A GLM models the mean of the response through a link function and can handle binary, count, and other non-Gaussian outcomes.

title: 3  Generalized linear models
text:
3.3 Why Do We Need GLMs?
Ordinary linear regression is not always appropriate.

For binary outcomes, fitted values from linear regression may fall outside \([0,1]\).
For count data, the response is nonnegative and often right-skewed.
The variance may depend on the mean, which violates the constant-variance assumption of linear regression.

GLMs address these issues by choosing a distribution and a link function suited to the data.

title: 3  Generalized linear models
text:
3.4 Common Examples

3.4.1 Linear Regression
For a Gaussian response with the identity link,
\[
\mu = X\beta.
\]
This is the ordinary linear model.


3.4.2 Logistic Regression
For binary data, logistic regression uses
\[
\log\left(\frac{\mu}{1-\mu}\right) = X\beta,
\]
where \(\mu = \mathbb{P}(Y=1 \mid X)\).


3.4.3 Poisson Regression
For count data, Poisson regression uses
\[
\log(\mu) = X\beta,
\]
where \(\mu = \mathbb{E}(Y \mid X)\).

title: 3  Generalized linear models
text:
3.5 Relation to Linear Regression
A GLM is not the same as transforming the response and then applying ordinary least squares.
For example,
\[
\log(Y) = X\beta + \varepsilon
\]
is a transformed linear model, whereas
\[
\log(\mathbb{E}[Y \mid X]) = X\beta
\]
is a GLM with a log link.
These models may look similar algebraically, but they differ in assumptions, interpretation, and estimation.

title: 3  Generalized linear models
3  Generalized linear models
text:
3.7 Interpretation
The coefficients in a GLM are interpreted through the link function.

In logistic regression, coefficients describe changes in log-odds.
In Poisson regression, coefficients describe changes in the log mean count.
In linear regression, coefficients describe direct changes in the mean response.

Therefore, interpretation depends on both the response distribution and the link.

title: 3  Generalized linear models
text:
3.8 Relation to GAMs and Regularization
GLMs still assume that the predictor enters through the linear form \(X\beta\). A generalized additive model (GAM) relaxes this by replacing linear terms with smooth functions:
\[
g(\mu) = \beta_0 + f_1(x_1) + \cdots + f_p(x_p).
\]
Regularization methods such as ridge regression and LASSO can also be combined with GLMs, producing penalized logistic regression, penalized Poisson regression, and related models.

title: 3  Generalized linear models
text:
3.9 Python Example
The following example fits a logistic regression model in Python using the public titanic dataset from seaborn. It includes coefficient tables, fitted probabilities, a classification table, and standard residual and influence diagnostics.

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import statsmodels.api as sm
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

import sys

print(sys.executable)

titanic = sns.load_dataset("titanic").dropna(subset=["survived", "fare", "age", "sex", "pclass"])

fit = smf.glm(
    "survived ~ fare + age + C(sex) + C(pclass)",
    data=titanic,
    family=sm.families.Binomial(),
).fit()

# Main GLM table
print(fit.summary())

# Coefficient table with confidence intervals
coef_table = fit.summary2().tables[1]
coef_table["odds_ratio"] = np.exp(coef_table["Coef."])
print(coef_table)

# Fitted probabilities and residual diagnostics
diagnostics = titanic[["survived", "fare", "age", "sex", "pclass"]].copy()
diagnostics["fitted_prob"] = fit.fittedvalues
diagnostics["pearson_resid"] = fit.resid_pearson
diagnostics["deviance_resid"] = fit.resid_deviance
diagnostics["response_resid"] = fit.resid_response
print(diagnostics.head())

# Simple classification table at cutoff 0.5
diagnostics["pred_class"] = (diagnostics["fitted_prob"] >= 0.5).astype(int)
print(pd.crosstab(diagnostics["survived"], diagnostics["pred_class"], margins=True))

# Influence summary
influence = fit.get_influence(observed=True)
print(influence.summary_frame().head())

# Diagnostic plots
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
ax.scatter(diagnostics["fitted_prob"], diagnostics["deviance_resid"], alpha=0.6)
ax.axhline(0, color="black", linestyle="--")
ax.set_xlabel("Fitted probability")
ax.set_ylabel("Deviance residual")
ax.set_title("Residuals vs fitted")
plt.tight_layout()

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
sm.qqplot(diagnostics["deviance_resid"], line="45", fit=True, ax=plt.gca())
plt.tight_layout()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
ax.scatter(influence.hat_matrix_diag, diagnostics["deviance_resid"], alpha=0.6)
ax.set_xlabel("Leverage")
ax.set_ylabel("Deviance residual")
ax.set_title("Residuals vs leverage")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: 3  Generalized linear models
section: 3.10 Similarities and Differences Between LM and GLM
text:
3.10 Similarities and Differences Between LM and GLM
A linear model (LM) and a generalized linear model (GLM) are closely related. In fact, the ordinary linear regression model can be viewed as a special case of a GLM.
They are similar in several important ways:

both relate a response variable to predictors;
both use a linear predictor of the form \(X\beta\);
both allow categorical variables, interactions, and transformed predictors;
both produce coefficient estimates, fitted values, residuals, and model summaries.

However, they differ in the way the response is modeled.
In a linear model, we write
\[
Y = X\beta + \varepsilon,
\]
with a Gaussian error assumption and constant variance.
In a GLM, we model the mean of the response through a link function:
\[
g(\mu) = X\beta, \qquad \mu = \mathbb{E}(Y \mid X).
\]
Thus, the major differences are:

an LM models the response directly;
a GLM models the mean of the response through a link function;
an LM usually assumes Gaussian errors with constant variance;
a GLM allows other response distributions such as Binomial or Poisson;
an LM is usually estimated by ordinary least squares;
a GLM is usually estimated by maximum likelihood.







NoteA Useful Way to Think About It



An LM is appropriate when the response is quantitative and approximately normal with constant variance. A GLM is appropriate when the response has a different distributional form, such as binary outcomes or counts, but we still want a regression model built from the linear predictor \(X\beta\).


The two models can also look algebraically similar. For example,
\[
\log(Y) = X\beta + \varepsilon
\]
is a transformed linear model, whereas
\[
\log(\mathbb{E}[Y \mid X]) = X\beta
\]
is a GLM with a log link. These are not the same model, even though the equations look similar. They differ in interpretation, assumptions, and estimation.
So the main relationship is:

an LM is a special case of a GLM;
a GLM keeps the linear predictor but generalizes the response distribution and the mean-response relationship.

title: 4  Generalized additive models
text:
4.1 What Is a GAM?
A generalized additive model (GAM) extends a generalized linear model by allowing the effect of a predictor to be a smooth function rather than a fixed linear term.
Instead of writing
\[
g(\mu) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_p x_p,
\]
a GAM writes
\[
g(\mu) = \beta_0 + f_1(x_1) + \cdots + f_p(x_p),
\]
where each \(f_j\) is a smooth function estimated from the data.
Here \(\mu = \mathbb{E}(Y \mid X)\) and \(g\) is a link function, just as in a GLM.

title: 4  Generalized additive models
text:
NoteMain Idea



A GLM assumes a specific parametric form for each predictor effect. A GAM allows the shape of the effect to be learned from the data, subject to a smoothness constraint.

title: 4  Generalized additive models
text:
4.2 Why Do We Need GAMs?
In practice, relationships between predictors and response are often nonlinear.
In ordinary linear regression, one may try transformations such as:

\(\log(x)\),
\(\sqrt{x}\),
\(x^2\).

These are still parametric choices made by the analyst. A GAM provides a more flexible alternative by estimating a smooth curve directly from the data.
This is useful when:

the true relationship is nonlinear;
the correct transformation is not known in advance;
we want flexibility without giving up interpretability completely.

title: 4  Generalized additive models
section: 4.3 Relation to Linear Regression and GLMs

title: 4  Generalized additive models
text:
4.4 Smooth Functions
The functions \(f_j\) are not arbitrary. They are usually represented by basis expansions, such as splines, and are estimated with a smoothness penalty.
The purpose of the penalty is to prevent the fitted curve from becoming too wiggly.
Thus, a GAM balances:

fidelity to the data,
smoothness of the fitted functions.

title: 4  Generalized additive models
text:
4.5 GAM Versus Manual Transformations
Suppose in a linear model you try
\[
Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \log(x) + \varepsilon
\]
or
\[
Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2 + \varepsilon.
\]
These are still linear models with chosen transformations.
In a GAM, by contrast, we write
\[
Y = \beta_0 + f(x) + \varepsilon
\]
or, more generally,
\[
g(\mu) = \beta_0 + f(x).
\]
The difference is that the shape of \(f\) is estimated from the data rather than fixed in advance as \(\log(x)\) or \(x^2\).

title: 4  Generalized additive models
text:
4.6 Estimation and Inference
GAMs are usually fitted by penalized likelihood methods. The amount of smoothness is controlled by smoothing parameters, which are often selected automatically by criteria such as:

generalized cross-validation (GCV),
restricted maximum likelihood (REML),
cross-validation.

Because the model learns functional form from the data, inference should still be interpreted with care, especially if the analyst repeatedly changes the model after inspecting diagnostic plots.

title: 4  Generalized additive models
5  Ridge Regression
text:
5.1 A Short History of Ridge Regression
Ridge regression was introduced as a remedy for instability in ordinary least squares when predictors are highly correlated. The idea of stabilizing an inverse problem by adding a penalty term originates from Tikhonov (1963) in numerical analysis, where a regularization term was added to ill-posed problems.
In statistics, Hoerl and Kennard (1970) formally introduced ridge regression to address multicollinearity in linear regression. Later work connected ridge regression to generalized inverses, biased estimation, Bayesian regression, and other regularization methods.

title: 5  Ridge Regression
text:
5.2 What Is Ridge Regression?
Ridge regression is a regularized version of ordinary least squares (OLS). It is especially useful when predictors are highly correlated or when the matrix \(X^\top X\) is nearly singular, because in such settings the OLS estimator can be unstable.
Recall the linear model
\[
y = X\beta + \varepsilon.
\]
When \(X^\top X\) is invertible, the OLS estimator is
\[
\hat{\beta}_{\mathrm{OLS}} = (X^\top X)^{-1}X^\top y.
\]
Ridge regression modifies the least-squares criterion by adding a quadratic penalty:
\[
\hat{\beta}_{\mathrm{ridge}}
= \arg\min_{\beta}
\left\{
\lVert y - X\beta \rVert_2^2 + \lambda \lVert \beta \rVert_2^2
\right\},
\]
where \(\lambda \ge 0\) is a tuning parameter.
Equivalently,
\[
\hat{\beta}_{\mathrm{ridge}} = (X^\top X + \lambda I)^{-1}X^\top y.
\]
The penalty shrinks coefficients toward zero. Larger values of \(\lambda\) produce stronger shrinkage.

title: 5  Ridge Regression
text:
5.3 Why Ridge Regression Helps
OLS is unbiased under the classical linear model assumptions, but it can have high variance when predictors are strongly correlated. Ridge regression introduces bias, but it often reduces variance enough to improve predictive performance.
This is a standard example of the bias-variance tradeoff.






NoteKey Properties




If \(\lambda = 0\), ridge regression reduces to OLS.
If \(\lambda > 0\), the matrix \(X^\top X + \lambda I\) is invertible even when \(X^\top X\) is singular.
Ridge shrinks coefficients continuously toward zero.
Ridge usually does not set coefficients exactly to zero.
Ridge is particularly useful under multicollinearity.

title: 5  Ridge Regression
text:
5.4 Choosing \(\lambda\)
The tuning parameter \(\lambda\) determines how much shrinkage is applied.

Small \(\lambda\) gives a model close to OLS.
Large \(\lambda\) gives stronger regularization.

In practice, the modern standard is to choose \(\lambda\) by cross-validation. If \(\mathrm{CV}(\lambda)\) denotes the cross-validation error, we choose
\[
\hat{\lambda}
= \arg\min_{\lambda} \mathrm{CV}(\lambda).
\]
Classical ridge analysis also uses ridge trace plots and generalized cross-validation.

title: 5  Ridge Regression
text:
5.5 Fitting Ridge Regression in R
The glmnet package is a standard implementation:
library(glmnet)

x <- model.matrix(y ~ ., data = dat)[, -1]
y <- dat$y

ridge_fit <- glmnet(x, y, alpha = 0)
plot(ridge_fit)
Cross-validation is performed with:
cv_fit <- cv.glmnet(x, y, alpha = 0)
plot(cv_fit)
coef(cv_fit, s = "lambda.min")
Here alpha = 0 specifies ridge regression.

title: 5  Ridge Regression
text:
5.6 Scaling Issues of Ridge Regression
Ridge regression is sensitive to the scale of the predictors, so the variables are usually standardized before fitting the model.
The usual standardization is
\[
x_j^* = \frac{x_j - \bar{x}_j}{s_j},
\]
so that each predictor has mean \(0\) and standard deviation \(1\).
This matters because the penalty acts directly on the coefficients. If predictors are measured on very different scales, then the penalty will not be applied fairly across variables.
In practice:

the predictors are standardized before fitting the model;
the intercept is typically left unpenalized;
software such as glmnet usually performs standardization automatically.

title: 5  Ridge Regression
text:
5.7 Relation to Tikhonov Regularization
Ridge regression is the statistical form of Tikhonov regularization. In inverse problems, Tikhonov regularization stabilizes an ill-posed problem by adding a quadratic penalty. Ridge regression applies the same idea to linear regression.

title: 5  Ridge Regression
5  Ridge Regression
text:
5.9 References

Hoerl, A. E., & Kennard, R. W. (1970). Ridge regression: Biased estimation for nonorthogonal problems. Technometrics, 12(1), 55-67.
Marquardt, D. W. (1970). Generalized inverses, ridge regression, biased linear estimation, and nonlinear estimation. Technometrics, 12(3), 591-612.
Tibshirani, R. (1996). Regression shrinkage and selection via the lasso. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society Series B, 58(1), 267-288.
Tikhonov, A. N. (1963). Solution of incorrectly formulated problems and the regularization method. Soviet Mathematics Doklady, 4, 1035-1038.

title: 6  LASSO
text:
6.1 What Is LASSO?
The LASSO is a regularized version of linear regression that adds an \(L_1\) penalty to the least-squares criterion. Its name stands for least absolute shrinkage and selection operator.
For a linear model with response vector \(y\) and design matrix \(X\), the LASSO estimator is defined by
\[
\hat{\beta}_{\mathrm{lasso}}
= \arg\min_{\beta}
\left\{
\lVert y - X\beta \rVert_2^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^p |\beta_j|
\right\},
\]
where \(\lambda \ge 0\) is a tuning parameter.
When \(\lambda = 0\), the method reduces to ordinary least squares. As \(\lambda\) increases, the penalty becomes stronger and the coefficients are shrunk more aggressively.

title: 6  LASSO
text:
NoteMain Point



Like ridge regression, LASSO controls model complexity by shrinking coefficients. Unlike ridge regression, it can set some coefficients exactly equal to zero, so it also performs variable selection.

title: 6  LASSO
6  LASSO
text:
6.3 Variable Selection and Shrinkage
The defining feature of LASSO is that the \(L_1\) penalty can force some coefficient estimates to be exactly zero.
As a result:

unimportant variables may be removed from the model;
the final model may be easier to interpret;
prediction error may improve if irrelevant variables are excluded.

Ridge regression also shrinks coefficients, but it usually keeps all variables in the model. LASSO shrinks and selects at the same time.

title: 6  LASSO
text:
6.4 Geometric Interpretation
The geometric explanation of LASSO is useful for understanding why exact zeros occur.
Ridge regression can be written with an \(L_2\) constraint:
\[
\sum_{j=1}^p \beta_j^2 \le t.
\]
The corresponding constraint region is a circle in two dimensions, or a sphere in higher dimensions.
LASSO can be written with an \(L_1\) constraint:
\[
\sum_{j=1}^p |\beta_j| \le t.
\]
The corresponding constraint region is a diamond in two dimensions.
Because the diamond has corners on the coordinate axes, the least-squares contours often touch the constraint region at a corner. At such a point, one or more coefficients are exactly zero.






ImportantComparison With Ridge



Ridge has a smooth constraint boundary, so coefficients are typically shrunk but remain nonzero. LASSO has corners aligned with the axes, so solutions often occur at points where some coefficients are exactly zero.

title: 6  LASSO
text:
6.5 Bias-Variance Tradeoff
LASSO is a biased estimator. The penalty pulls coefficients toward zero, so the estimates are no longer unbiased in the classical sense.
However, this bias can be worthwhile because it may greatly reduce variance. If the reduction in variance is large enough, the overall prediction error can decrease.
Thus, LASSO is another example of the bias-variance tradeoff:

less flexibility may increase bias;
less flexibility may also reduce variance;
prediction may improve if the tradeoff is favorable.

title: 6  LASSO
text:
6.6 How to Choose \(\lambda\)
The tuning parameter \(\lambda\) controls the amount of regularization in both ridge regression and LASSO.

When \(\lambda = 0\), both methods reduce to ordinary least squares.
As \(\lambda\) increases, the coefficients are shrunk more strongly.
In ridge regression, coefficients move continuously toward zero.
In LASSO, some coefficients may become exactly zero.

So the practical question is: how should we choose \(\lambda\)?






NoteMain Idea



There is no single universally best value of \(\lambda\). It is usually chosen to balance model fit and model complexity, with the goal of improving prediction and avoiding overfitting.



6.6.1 A Simple Way
The simplest explanation is to treat \(\lambda\) as a knob that controls the strength of shrinkage.

A small \(\lambda\) gives a model close to least squares.
A large \(\lambda\) gives a more heavily regularized model.

In an introductory discussion, one can fit the model over a grid of \(\lambda\) values and inspect how the coefficients change. This gives an immediate visual understanding of the regularization path.
For ridge regression, the coefficients decrease smoothly as \(\lambda\) grows. For LASSO, some coefficients drop to zero as \(\lambda\) increases.


6.6.2 The Traditional Classical Way
A classical approach is to choose \(\lambda\) using an analytic criterion that balances goodness of fit against model complexity.
For ridge regression, older literature often used:

inspection of ridge trace plots,
Mallows’ \(C_p\),
Akaike’s Information Criterion (AIC),
Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC),
generalized cross-validation (GCV).

For LASSO, information criteria such as AIC or BIC can also be used, usually together with an estimate of the effective degrees of freedom.


6.6.3 The Modern Way
The modern standard is to choose \(\lambda\) by cross-validation, especially \(K\)-fold cross-validation.
If \(\mathrm{CV}(\lambda)\) denotes the cross-validation error, we choose
\[
\hat{\lambda}
= \arg\min_{\lambda} \mathrm{CV}(\lambda).
\]
In practice, two values are often reported:

\(\lambda_{\min}\), the value that minimizes cross-validation error;
\(\lambda_{1se}\), the largest value of \(\lambda\) within one standard error of the minimum.

Usually, \(\lambda_{\min}\) gives the most accurate prediction, while \(\lambda_{1se}\) gives a simpler and more stable model.

title: 6  LASSO
text:
6.7 Standardization
For both ridge regression and LASSO, the predictors are usually standardized before fitting the model.
The usual standardization is
\[
x_j^* = \frac{x_j - \bar{x}_j}{s_j},
\]
so that each predictor has mean \(0\) and standard deviation \(1\).
This is important because the penalty acts on the coefficient magnitudes. Without standardization, variables measured on different scales would be penalized unequally.
In practice:

we usually standardize the predictors \(X\);
we usually do not penalize the intercept;
software such as glmnet standardizes predictors automatically by default.

title: 6  LASSO
text:
6.8 Fitting LASSO With glmnet
In R, the most common implementation is the glmnet package.
library(glmnet)

x <- model.matrix(y ~ ., data = dat)[, -1]
y <- dat$y

lasso_fit <- glmnet(x, y, alpha = 1)
plot(lasso_fit)
To choose \(\lambda\) by cross-validation:
cv_fit <- cv.glmnet(x, y, alpha = 1)
plot(cv_fit)

coef(cv_fit, s = "lambda.min")
coef(cv_fit, s = "lambda.1se")
Here:

alpha = 1 specifies LASSO;
lambda.min gives the model with minimum CV error;
lambda.1se gives a simpler model within one standard error of the minimum.

title: 6  LASSO
section: 6.9 Relation to Forward Stagewise Regression and Elastic Net
text:
6.9 Relation to Forward Stagewise Regression and Elastic Net
LASSO is closely related to forward stagewise regression because both build a model gradually and often produce similar coefficient paths.
LASSO is also closely related to the elastic net, which combines the ridge and LASSO penalties:
\[
\lambda_1 \sum_{j=1}^p |\beta_j| + \lambda_2 \sum_{j=1}^p \beta_j^2.
\]
Elastic net is often useful when predictors are highly correlated, because it combines variable selection with additional shrinkage.

title: 6  LASSO
Appendix A — Quick intro to R
text: A.1 For those who are from other languages, especially Python

title: Appendix A — Quick intro to R
text:
The character . is NOT special. It doesn’t mean packages or classes. It is just one of the characters that can be used as variable names.
R recommends to use <- to denote assigning values, although = is accepted. a <- 1 and a = 1 are essentially the same. It has some benefits
